text.title: Solo Pivot Decision — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules governing pivot classification, hypothesis design, and the boundary between pivot, optimise, and quit.

rule.statement: Classify the proposed pivot into EXACTLY ONE of: SEGMENT (different buyer), FEATURE (different value), BUSINESS-MODEL (different revenue), TECH (different stack), CHANNEL (different distribution). Multi-axis pivots are forbidden — execute one at a time.

rationale.source: Eric Ries — The Lean Startup, condensed by Dan Olsen

rule.rationale: Multi-axis pivots are statistically indistinguishable from quitting and starting over. The single-axis constraint preserves learning from v1.

rule.statement: Every pivot must be accompanied by a written falsifiable hypothesis: "If we <change>, then within <N> days <metric> will improve from <baseline> to <target>. If it does not, we kill the pivot."
rule.rationale: Without a kill-criterion the pivot becomes a perpetual project. Pre-committed thresholds force the founder to update beliefs on real data, not vibes.

rule.statement: The sunk-cost statement MUST list (a) money spent, (b) time spent, (c) what LEARNING survives the pivot. Pivots that preserve no learning from v1 are quits, not pivots.

rationale.source: Mihail Eric / Lean Startup Lab cohort data

rule.rationale: Founders confuse "I built code" with "I learned something". A pivot's value is in the learning, not the code. Surfacing surviving learnings is a hard test of pivot legitimacy.

rule.statement: Required runway = max(8 weeks, 2× the original v1 build time). If operator runway is below this gate, the framework returns `recommended_action: sunset` regardless of pivot quality.
rule.rationale: Pivots executed with insufficient runway fail at ~85%. Failing the pivot costs the founder the option to start something else from a position of strength.

rule.statement: If the proposed pivot is FEATURE + SEGMENT + BUSINESS-MODEL simultaneously, the framework MUST relabel it as "quit and restart" and route to founder-mental-health resources. Pivot is a course correction; restart is starting fresh — they need different decisions.

rationale.source: Common founder failure pattern (Sahil Lavingia, Justin Jackson essays)

rule.rationale: The classic "I'll pivot from B2C consumer mobile to B2B enterprise developer tools" is a restart that founders call a pivot to avoid grief. Naming it correctly improves the next decision.

example.title: SEGMENT pivot (one axis)
example.code:
v1: time-tracking for freelancers (Y = freelancers) v2: time-tracking for agencies (Y = small agencies)
X (role) unchanged. Z (need) unchanged: "stop losing billable hours." Y changed → SEGMENT pivot. Valid single-axis.
example.note: Hypothesis: "Within 60 days, $1k+ contracts will replace $9/mo subs, MRR up 3×."

example.title: Triple pivot (r5 trigger)
example.code:
v1: SaaS for freelancer time-tracking, $9/mo. v2: AI agent platform for enterprises, $50k/yr SOW.
SEGMENT changed (freelancer → enterprise) FEATURE changed (time-tracking → AI agents) BUSINESS-MODEL changed (SaaS → SOW)
Framework relabels as RESTART. Not a pivot.
example.note: Illustrates r5. Restarts can be legitimate, but they require a different decision process (closing v1 cleanly, refunding, retaining/firing users).
